---
title: Time-series Forecasting in Safety-critical Environments
authors:
  - name: Thomas Bartz-Beielstein
    affiliations:
      - name: Bartz & Bartz GmbH, 51643 Gummersbach, Germany
    orcid: 0000-0002-5938-5158
    email: bartzbeielstein@gmail.com
    url: https://www.spotseven.de
    corresponding: true
    roles: writing, conceptualization, supervision
  - name: Eva Bartz
    affiliation: Bartz & Bartz GmbH, 51643 Gummersbach, Germany
    email: eva.bartz@bartzundbartz.de
    roles: writing
runninghead: "spotforecast2-safe"
abstract: |
    The `spotforecast2-safe` package provides a deterministic, auditable subset of the broader `spotforecast2` forecasting stack [@spotforecast2], intended for deployment in regulated environments such as those covered by the European Union Artificial Intelligence Act (EU AI Act), IEC 61508 (functional safety), ISO 26262 (road vehicle functional safety), and the ISA/IEC 62443 series for cybersecurity in industrial automation and control systems, which the forthcoming EU Cyber Resilience Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/2847) is expected to ratify as a harmonised standard for products with digital elements. It implements recursive multi-step forecasting, cyclic and calendar exogenous features, quantile binning, linear interpolation with explicit contracts, and rolling-origin backtesting. Using SPDX-header information, every line of code has a transparent provenance. Four non-negotiable code-level engineering rules govern the package: zero dead code, deterministic transformations, fail-safe processing (invalid input raises rather than being silently repaired), and a minimal dependency footprint. A parallel set of four process-level rules --- traceability, a documented threat model, a signed supply chain, and a structured audit log --- is satisfied through `MODEL_CARD.md`, CI workflows, and the release pipeline. Plotting, AutoML, and deep-learning backends are deliberately excluded because each inflates the attack surface, introduces nondeterminism, or breaks reproducibility across platforms. This report documents the architecture, formalizes the preprocessing and forecasting algorithms, maps design choices onto EU AI Act transparency and robustness articles, presents a worked end-to-end electricity-load example, and describes the audit trail built from executable docstrings, a pinned Common Platform Enumeration (CPE) identifier, and REUSE-compliant licensing. The package is open source under AGPL-3.0-or-later.

    \textbf{Keywords:} Time-series forecasting, Safety-critical machine learning, EU AI Act, Deterministic computing, skforecast, spotforecast2
jupyter: spotforecast2-safe
echo: true
reference-section-title: References
header-includes: |
  \usepackage{dirtree}
  \DefineVerbatimEnvironment{Highlighting}{Verbatim}{commandchars=\\\{\},fontsize=\small}
format:
  arxiv-pdf:
    keep-tex: true
    mainfont: "TeX Gyre Termes"
    mathfont: "TeX Gyre Termes Math"
    number-sections: true
    toc: false
    classoption:
      - onecolumn
---

# Introduction {#sec-introduction}

Machine-learning practice and regulated engineering have, until recently, occupied largely disjoint software ecosystems. The forecasting research community favours feature-rich libraries that integrate plotting, hyperparameter search, probabilistic deep-learning backends, and interactive dashboards into a single installable package. This tooling is excellent for exploration and reproducible research, but each convenience is also a cost once the resulting model is deployed into a setting that must withstand external audit. A plotting dependency adds a GPU driver requirement and several hundred megabytes of C extensions. An AutoML layer introduces run-to-run variation that complicates post-hoc incident investigation. A deep-learning backend brings with it a tensor library whose application binary interface (ABI) changes between minor releases, breaking long-term reproducibility of the very artefacts that regulators ask to inspect.

The EU AI Act [@euAI24] codifies the distinction. Articles 10, 13, and 15 require high-risk systems to provide evidence of data-governance practices, transparency to downstream deployers, and an appropriate level of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity. A demonstrator built on top of a stack that pulls in dozens of unpinned transitive dependencies is not impossible to certify, but the cost of establishing the mandatory audit trail grows with every dependency whose behaviour is unconstrained by the package vendor. Pre-existing functional-safety standards --- IEC 61508-3 for electrical and electronic systems [@iec61508] and the road-vehicle adaptation ISO 26262-6 [@iso26262] --- make the same point from the other direction: software whose behaviour depends on uncontrolled externalities cannot be classified above Safety Integrity Level (SIL) 1 or Automotive Safety Integrity Level (ASIL) A without a disproportionate verification effort.

Functional safety and AI transparency are not the only regulatory vectors. In industrial deployments a forecasting library runs as a software component at Level 2 or 3 of the Purdue reference model (ISA-95 / IEC 62264), where the dominant cybersecurity framework is the ISA/IEC 62443 series [@iec62443_1_1]. The series partitions responsibility across asset owners (62443-2-1 [@iec62443_2_1]), service providers (62443-2-4 [@iec62443_2_4]), system integrators (62443-3-2 [@iec62443_3_2], 62443-3-3 [@iec62443_3_3]), and component manufacturers (62443-4-1 [@iec62443_4_1], 62443-4-2 [@iec62443_4_2]) --- the last two are directly applicable to a library vendor. Within the EU, 62443 is additionally the leading candidate harmonised standard for the Cyber Resilience Act (CRA) [@euCRA24] and informs risk-management obligations under the Network and Information Security 2 (NIS2) Directive [@euNIS22] and the Critical Entities Resilience Directive [@euRCE22]. @fluc24a observes that 62443-2-1 is "the equivalent of ISO/IEC 27001 for cyber-physical systems (CPS)"; the part most directly binding on `spotforecast2-safe` is however 62443-4-1, whose Secure Product Development Lifecycle requirements map closely onto our four engineering rules. NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3 [@nist80082r3] provides a complementary US-federal reference with substantial technical overlap.

The package `spotforecast2-safe` is our response to this tension. Its starting point is `skforecast` [@scip24a], a well-documented forecasting library that implements the recursive multi-step approach, lag and window features, direct multi-output forecasting, and rolling-origin cross-validation. Approximately twenty-nine per cent of the safety-critical package by line count is a direct port of `skforecast` source, with the original BSD-3-Clause copyright retained and the dual licence AGPL-3.0-or-later added to record the modification. Additional code enforces four code-level engineering rules. First, zero dead code: every public function is reached by at least one executable docstring example or a test. Second, deterministic transformations: no unseeded random number generators, no dictionary iteration order dependencies, no parallelism that fails to pin `n_jobs`. Third, fail-safe processing: NaN values, wrong dtypes, or missing indices raise explicit `ValueError` or `TypeError`, and the caller must opt in to silent imputation by passing a typed flag. Fourth, minimal footprint: a short, enforced blocklist of prohibited dependencies (see @sec-principles) eliminates plotting, AutoML, and deep-learning transitive closures. A parallel set of process-level rules --- traceability, a documented threat model, a signed supply chain, and a structured audit log --- sits alongside these and is satisfied at review and release time rather than at commit time.

These rules have a cost in features. `spotforecast2-safe` is therefore distributed as the safety-critical subset of a more permissive sibling package, `spotforecast2` [@spotforecast2], developed by the same author under the same AGPL-3.0-or-later licence. The upstream package retains the full forecasting stack that was trimmed for this report: interactive visualisation based on `plotly` and `matplotlib`, AutoML hyperparameter search via `optuna` and `spotoptim`, model-explanation tooling based on `shap`, and Jupyter integration. A deployer who does not face the EU AI Act, IEC 61508, ISO 26262, or ISA/IEC 62443 is expected to use `spotforecast2`; `spotforecast2-safe` is explicitly an artefact for regulated deployment rather than a drop-in replacement for the upstream. The remainder of this report describes the subset. Readers looking for the full feature surface should consult the upstream documentation linked from the bibliography entry.

This report has three goals. First, it positions the package within the landscape of Python time-series tools and explains which features are deliberately absent from the safety-critical subset and why (@sec-related). Second, it documents the architecture, the formal preprocessing and forecasting algorithms, and the safety engineering decisions shipped in recent releases (@sec-principles through @sec-safety). Third, it maps those design decisions onto specific clauses of the EU AI Act and the two functional-safety standards, and closes with a worked end-to-end electricity-load example (@sec-compliance and @sec-example). The report is itself an artefact of the audit story: every code block is executed during document render, the bibliography is under version control, and the CPE identifier assigned to the package is pinned by a dedicated test.

# Related Work {#sec-related}

The Python forecasting ecosystem has matured rapidly over the past decade. The scientific baseline is set by `statsmodels`, which provides ARIMA, SARIMAX, and exponential-smoothing models following the canonical treatment of @box15a. `scikit-learn` [@pedr11a] offers a wide estimator catalogue but intentionally omits time-series-aware cross-validation primitives, delegating that responsibility to downstream libraries. On top of these foundations several forecasting-specific tools have emerged.

`skforecast` [@scip24a] provides a `Forecaster` abstraction that wraps any scikit-learn-compatible regressor into a recursive, direct, or multi-series forecaster. It is the closest conceptual relative of the package documented here. We reuse its recursive-forecasting architecture, its rolling-origin splitter, and large parts of its `_binner`, `_differentiator`, and residuals plumbing. We do not reuse its plotting subsystem (which depends on `matplotlib` and `plotly`) or its AutoML-style `bayesian_search_forecaster` (which depends on `optuna` and introduces nondeterministic optimisation traces).

The Nixtla ecosystem contributes three complementary libraries. `statsforecast` [@garz22a] provides vectorised implementations of classical models, trading model flexibility for aggressive runtime optimisation via Numba [@lam15a]. `mlforecast` extends the same API to gradient-boosted machines. `neuralforecast` exposes deep-learning architectures (N-BEATS, N-HiTS, PatchTST, and numerous transformer variants) via a unified interface. The deep-learning backend violates the minimal-footprint rule directly, and the underlying PyTorch dependency propagates a CUDA requirement that is inappropriate on many embedded targets.

`Darts` [@herz22a] presents a unified API for classical and deep models, but its installation footprint is dominated by optional dependencies whose behaviour influences availability of model classes at import time, an anti-pattern for a reproducible build. `tsai`, `Merlion`, and `Kats` occupy similar territory with various degrees of dependency burden.

The M-competitions [@makr20a; @makr22a] have become the de facto benchmark for point and interval forecasting and established LightGBM [@ke17a], XGBoost [@chen16a], and ensembles thereof as strong baselines across domains. Our choice to limit the package's supported backends to LightGBM, XGBoost, and scikit-learn regressors (and to ship `ForecasterRecursiveLGBM` / `ForecasterRecursiveXGB` as the two default model bundles) follows directly from that empirical evidence.

None of the libraries above is poorly engineered. Several are excellent pieces of research software. The distinction is one of purpose: a library optimised for rapid exploration cannot simultaneously meet a SIL-3 software-verification budget without extensive, downstream, per-deployer rework. `spotforecast2-safe` moves that rework into the package itself, so that a deployer inherits the evidence rather than having to reproduce it.

A parallel line of work establishes *how* trustworthiness of a high-risk AI system should be assessed. @stet24a propose a trustworthiness-assurance framework that decomposes the EU AI Act requirements into verifiable properties along the V-model, connecting AI system engineering to the functional-safety assurance case. The present report is compatible with that framing: each process-level rule in @sec-principles is an argument step, and the mechanisms and evidence columns of @tbl-compliance are the substantiations. The legal backdrop is surveyed by @raue25a, whose volume on *Artificial Intelligence and Fundamental Rights* analyses the Regulation's interaction with the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union, non-discrimination law, and data-protection law, and is the companion reference when a deployer needs to translate the engineering evidence documented here into a fundamental-rights impact assessment (FRIA) as required by Article 27 EU-AI Act.

A separate line of the same author's work, the upstream package `spotforecast2` [@spotforecast2], occupies a different position in this space. It retains the `skforecast`-like ergonomics --- plotting, AutoML, and SHAP-based explainability --- rather than moving the verification cost into the library, and is the recommended tool when the deployment is not regulated.

# Design Principles {#sec-principles}

The design principles separate into two parallel lists. *Code-level engineering rules* constrain what the source code may contain and are enforced at commit time by a test or lint. *Process-level rules* constrain how the package is developed, released, and operated, and are satisfied through `MODEL_CARD.md`, CI workflows, and the release pipeline rather than through source-code tests. Both lists are necessary: the code-level rules alone do not satisfy the regulatory obligations for traceability, threat modelling, data governance, supply-chain integrity, or operational observability.

## Code-level engineering rules

The four non-negotiable code-level rules, each stated in a single line and enforced by a specific mechanism, are:

1. *Zero dead code.* No function, class, or branch is shipped without a test or an executable docstring. A `test_docstring_examples_*.py` suite enforces this.
2. *Deterministic transformations.* Same input, same bit-level output, across Python minor versions and operating systems. Random-number generators are seeded; dictionary iteration is not relied upon; `joblib` parallelism is pinned to a fixed `n_jobs`.
3. *Fail-safe processing.* Invalid input raises an explicit exception rather than being silently imputed or coerced. Silent imputation is available only behind a `Literal["raise", "ffill_bfill", "passthrough"]` opt-in flag.
4. *Minimal Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE) surface.* A short, version-controlled blocklist of prohibited dependencies (`plotly`, `matplotlib`, `spotoptim`, `optuna`, `torch`, `tensorflow`) is enforced by a test that grep-scans the lockfile. Runtime dependencies are restricted to a curated subset of widely used packages whose maintainers publish security advisories.

These rules are not aesthetic preferences. Each corresponds to a specific failure mode observed during internal deployment reviews. Dead code has been found to hide bit-rotted branches that raise at runtime on unusual inputs; deterministic transformations are necessary for a regulator to reproduce a claimed metric from a reported seed; fail-safe processing prevents NaN-contamination of a forecast pipeline from becoming an undetected data-quality incident; and the minimal-footprint rule has prevented the package from inheriting at least one high-severity CVE during 2025 alone (the `protobuf`/`grpcio` chain, which would have arrived via any of TensorFlow, PyTorch, or Ray).

The rules interact. Deterministic transformations enable the executable docstring tests to be effective: a docstring example with a `doctest`-style expected output only serves as a test if the output is reproducible. Fail-safe processing supports determinism by refusing to silently coerce a dtype into something that would later influence a downstream hash. And the minimal-footprint rule keeps the grep-scan cheap enough to run on every commit.

These rules also recover the secure-development practices required by IEC 62443-4-1 [@iec62443_4_1], which defines eight security-management process areas --- Security Management (SM), Specification of Security Requirements (SR), Secure by Design (SD), Secure Implementation (SI), Security Verification and Validation (SVV), Management of Security-related Issues (DM), Security Update Management (SUM), and Security Guidelines (SG) --- at four maturity levels (ML 1 *Initial* through ML 4 *Improving*). The rule "zero dead code" instantiates SM-1 (security management process) and SI-1 (coding guidelines with enforcement); "deterministic transformations" instantiates SVV-1 and SVV-3 (reproducible verification); "fail-safe processing" instantiates SR-2 (threat-model-driven requirements) and DM-1 (defect identification); and the minimal-footprint rule instantiates SM-7 (security of third-party components). The Security Development Lifecycle (SDL) maturity the package currently evidences is ML 2 *Managed*; ML 3 *Defined* requires a formal threat model, which is listed as future work in @sec-outlook.

## Process-level rules

Four further rules govern how the package is developed, released, and operated. None of them is enforceable by a pytest assertion against source code; each is satisfied by an artefact --- `MODEL_CARD.md`, a CI workflow, the semantic-release pipeline, or a logging schema --- and checked at review time or at release time. The decomposition mirrors the V-model-based trustworthiness-assurance structure proposed by @stet24a, in which AI-specific regulatory requirements are refined into verifiable properties whose evidence is collected along the development lifecycle.

1. *Traceability.* Every public symbol and every non-trivial private helper links back to a documented requirement in `MODEL_CARD.md` (2.--5.) or to a specific clause of the compliance table in @sec-compliance. This satisfies IEC 61508-3 7.2 (software safety requirements specification) [@iec61508], ISO 26262-6 7.4.2 (ASIL-dependent requirements derivation) [@iso26262], and EU AI Act Art. 9 (risk management across the lifecycle) [@euAI24]. Orphan symbols --- those with neither a requirement link nor a `MODEL_CARD` entry --- are flagged in code review rather than by CI.
2. *Documented threat model.* A STRIDE table per network-facing module (`downloader/entsoe.py`, `weather/weather_client.py`) is maintained alongside the code; any change to the attack surface requires an updated threat entry in the same pull request. This satisfies IEC 62443-4-1 SR-1 and SR-2 (threat-model-driven security requirements) [@iec62443_4_1] and closes the ML-2 $\rightarrow$ ML-3 gap admitted in @sec-outlook.
3. *Signed supply chain.* Every release ships a signed PyPI wheel and a signed Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) attestation, both published to the `sigstore` transparency log alongside the GitHub Release. The signed wheel satisfies IEC 62443-4-1 SUM-3 (secure update delivery) [@iec62443_4_1] and the corresponding delivery-integrity requirement in EU Cyber Resilience Act Annex I Part I [@euCRA24]. The signed SBOM attestation satisfies EU Cyber Resilience Act Annex I Part II (vulnerability-handling obligations), whose point (1) explicitly requires producing a software bill of materials. The input side of the supply chain is already covered by code-level rule 4.
4. *Structured audit log.* Every operational action emits a log record following a pinned JSON schema under `~/spotforecast2_safe_models/logs/`; the schema is versioned and any change is a Conventional-Commits `feat!:` subject that triggers a major version bump. This satisfies IEC 62443-4-2 SAR 6.1 (audit log accessibility) [@iec62443_4_2] and IEC 61508-3 7.4.7 (verification of the integrated software) [@iec61508], which both require operational traceability beyond development-time tests.

A further separate rule concerns data governance: the package itself ships no training data, but every forecaster object records the provenance of the data it was fitted on (source URL, retrieval timestamp, content hash) in its persisted state. This satisfies the provenance requirement of EU AI Act Art. 10 (data and data governance) [@euAI24], which is as far as a library can go without seeing the deployer's actual training data. Assessing representativity, bias, and fitness for the intended use case remains the deployer's responsibility and is documented in the deployer's own `MODEL_CARD.md`. We treat this as a refinement of the traceability rule rather than a fifth process rule, because the mechanism (provenance recorded in a persisted artefact) is a special case of requirement-to-implementation linkage.

A further principle, not strictly one of the four but a consequence of them, is that every source file carries an SPDX header in the format recommended by REUSE v3.0 [@reuse30]. Ported `skforecast` files carry both the original BSD-3-Clause notice and the added AGPL-3.0-or-later, so that the licence resolution is a dual-licence derivative and not an attempt to re-licence another project's code.

# Architecture {#sec-architecture}

The package is organised into three layers. The lowest layer (`forecaster/`, `preprocessing/`, `model_selection/`, `data/`) contains the estimator wrappers, transformers, and splitters that most directly descend from `skforecast`. A middle layer (`manager/`) wraps an estimator together with an `ExogBuilder`, a configurator, a logger, and persistence into a single unit of deployment. The top layer (`processing/` and `tasks/`) provides end-to-end pipelines that compose the lower two layers into console scripts.

\dirtree{%
.1 src/spotforecast2\_safe/.
.2 forecaster/.
.3 recursive/.
.3 base.py, utils.py, metrics.py.
.2 preprocessing/.
.3 repeating\_basis\_function.py.
.3 \_binner.py, \_differentiator.py.
.3 curate\_data.py, imputation.py.
.3 linearly\_interpolate\_ts.py.
.3 outlier.py, split.py.
.2 model\_selection/.
.3 split\_ts\_cv.py, split\_one\_step.py.
.3 validation.py.
.2 manager/.
.3 models/, configurator/, exo/.
.3 persistence.py, trainer.py, predictor.py.
.2 processing/, tasks/.
.2 downloader/, weather/.
.2 utils/, exceptions.py.
}

The public API is pinned by `__all__` in the top-level package and currently contains eleven symbols: `Period`, `RepeatingBasisFunction`, `ExogBuilder`, `LinearlyInterpolateTS`, three `ForecasterRecursive*` model classes, `ConfigEntsoe`, plus the alias `Config` and the two utility members `__version__` and `hello`. Any breaking change to these symbols requires a `feat!:` conventional-commit subject and triggers a semantic-release major version bump. Private modules (anything prefixed with an underscore or living under a submodule not re-exported at the top level) are explicitly unstable and exempt from this contract.

Adding a new estimator wrapper thus takes place in the `manager/models/` directory: the wrapper inherits from `ForecasterRecursiveModel`, which itself orchestrates `fit`, `predict`, `backtest`, and `save/load`. The wrapper is responsible only for declaring the backend regressor and any backend-specific serialisation hooks. The exogenous-feature layer, the rolling-origin validation, and the audit logging are inherited.

## Relation to `spotforecast2` {#sec-architecture-relation}

The package shares its core with the upstream sibling `spotforecast2` [@spotforecast2]: the `forecaster/` recursive machinery, the `preprocessing/` transformers, the `model_selection/` time-aware splitters, and the `manager/` orchestration layer are common to both. The safe subset omits exactly those upstream modules that depend on a prohibited dependency, a nondeterministic tuning loop, or tooling whose reproducibility guarantees have not been verified. @tbl-spotforecast2-diff lists the concrete omissions. Conversely, the safe subset adds four subpackages that do not exist in the upstream --- `datasets/`, `downloader/`, `processing/`, and `weather/` --- because they codify the ENTSO-E and weather-data ingestion path that the audit story of @sec-safety relies on.

The dependency direction is one-way. `spotforecast2-safe` does not import from `spotforecast2`; it is installable and testable in isolation. `spotforecast2` re-exports selected public symbols from `spotforecast2-safe` (notably `ConfigEntsoe`), so that a deployer who chooses the upstream package still sees a consistent configuration surface. This arrangement lets the safe subset be released on its own cadence and certified independently, while the upstream remains a strict superset for non-regulated use. Note that SHAP, Jupyter, and IPython are not on the enforced blocklist of @sec-principles; they are omitted from the safe subset because their outputs are not bit-reproducible across minor versions, not because a regulation forbids them.

```{=latex}
\begin{table}[ht]
\centering
\footnotesize
\caption{\label{tbl-spotforecast2-diff}Modules and runtime dependencies present in the upstream package but omitted from the safe subset, with the triggering dependency or rationale.}
\begin{tabular}{p{0.30\columnwidth} p{0.20\columnwidth} p{0.40\columnwidth}}
\hline
\textbf{Upstream module or dependency} & \textbf{Trigger} & \textbf{Rationale} \\
\hline
\texttt{manager/plotter.py} & \texttt{plotly} & Interactive plotting is a prohibited dependency; the safe subset emits pandas DataFrames for the caller's plotting library of choice. \\
\texttt{preprocessing/time\_series\_visualization.py} & \texttt{matplotlib}, \texttt{plotly} & As above. \\
\texttt{preprocessing/outlier\_plots.py} & \texttt{matplotlib}, \texttt{plotly} & As above. \\
\texttt{model\_selection/bayesian\_search.py} & \texttt{optuna} & Bayesian optimisation introduces run-to-run variation that complicates reproducibility claims. \\
\texttt{model\_selection/spotoptim\_search.py} & \texttt{spotoptim} & Same rationale; surrogate-model-based tuning is out of scope. \\
\texttt{model\_selection/grid\_search.py}, \texttt{random\_search.py} & Footprint & Exhaustive and random search are deterministic but would enlarge the \texttt{manager/} surface without a corresponding safety gain. \\
\texttt{manager/models/forecaster\_recursive\_model\_full.py} & \texttt{shap} & Model-explanation output is not required by the audit trail and is not bit-reproducible across \texttt{shap} minor versions. \\
\texttt{manager/models/forecaster\_recursive\_xgb\_full.py} & Extended features & The safe subset ships a lean \texttt{ForecasterRecursiveXGB} without the explanation hooks. \\
\texttt{manager/multitask/spotoptim.py} & \texttt{spotoptim} & Multi-objective tuning is out of scope. \\
\texttt{stats/} subpackage & Determinism & Analytical utilities whose determinism across platforms has not yet been verified. \\
\texttt{jupyter}, \texttt{ipykernel} runtime deps & Footprint & Moved to the development-only dependency group. \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
```

# Preprocessing and Features {#sec-preprocessing}

The `preprocessing` subpackage contains four kinds of transformers. Cyclic encoders render periodic calendar features (hour-of-day, day-of-week, day-of-year) into a smooth representation that a gradient-boosted machine can use without introducing an artefact at the year boundary. Interpolators reconstruct short gaps under an explicit contract. A quantile binner discretises a continuous feature into equal-frequency buckets. A differentiator applies a finite-difference operator for stationarisation.

The flagship cyclic encoder is `RepeatingBasisFunction`, based on the repeating radial basis function (RBF) construction. It places $K$ Gaussian basis functions around a normalised unit circle and wraps the distance metric around the period. For an input $t$ with period $T$ and basis-function centres $c_1, \ldots, c_K$ uniformly spaced on $[0, 1)$, define $\theta(t) = (t - t_{\min}) / (t_{\max} - t_{\min})$ and the wrapped distance
$$
d_k(t) = \min\bigl(|\theta(t) - c_k|,\, 1 - |\theta(t) - c_k|\bigr).
$$
The $k$-th feature is then
$$
\phi_k(t) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{d_k(t)^2}{2 \sigma^2}\right), \qquad \sigma = 1/K.
$$

The bandwidth $\sigma = 1/K$ matches the uniform spacing of centres and provides a smooth, overlap-complete encoding: the partition-of-unity property holds approximately for $K \ge 4$ and the sum of activations is roughly constant over $[0, 1)$. @fig-rbf visualises the resulting basis.

```{python}
#| label: fig-rbf
#| fig-cap: "Repeating Basis Function kernels across an hour-of-day cycle ($K=6$ basis functions, period 24 hours). The sum of activations (dashed black line) is approximately constant, giving the encoder the partition-of-unity property that makes it well-suited for tree-based regressors."
#| echo: true
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", message="IProgress not found")

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from spotforecast2_safe.preprocessing.repeating_basis_function import (
    RepeatingBasisFunction,
)

hours = np.linspace(0, 23, 500)
df = pd.DataFrame({"hour": hours})
rbf = RepeatingBasisFunction(n_periods=6, column="hour", input_range=(0, 23))
features = rbf.fit_transform(df)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.5, 2.8))
for k in range(features.shape[1]):
    ax.plot(hours, features[:, k], linewidth=1.2)
ax.plot(hours, features.sum(axis=1), "k--", linewidth=1.0, label="sum")
ax.set_xlabel("hour of day")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$\phi_k(t)$")
ax.set_xlim(0, 23)
ax.set_ylim(-0.05, features.sum(axis=1).max() * 1.05)
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Three further transformers complete the preprocessing layer. `LinearlyInterpolateTS` fills short gaps with the `on_missing: Literal["raise", "ffill_bfill", "passthrough"]` opt-in contract introduced in PR #112; the default value `"raise"` implements the fail-safe rule. `QuantileBinner` computes equal-frequency bin edges on the training set and uses `numpy.searchsorted` to assign positions at transform time, avoiding the pandas `qcut` object creation cost on hot paths. `TimeSeriesDifferentiator` applies an $m$-order backward difference, $\nabla^m y_t = \sum_{j=0}^{m} \binom{m}{j} (-1)^{j} y_{t-j}$, and exposes an `inverse_transform` that reconstructs the original series given the pre-difference anchor values.

All four transformers subclass `sklearn.base.TransformerMixin` and therefore compose into a `Pipeline`. The pipeline's `get_feature_names_out` contract is preserved, so that downstream model cards can report the exact feature vector reaching the regressor.

# Recursive Forecasting {#sec-forecasting}

The forecasting layer implements the recursive multi-step strategy: a single scalar one-step-ahead model is trained and then iterated to produce horizons of arbitrary length [@hynd21a]. For a target series $y_t$, lags $\mathcal{L} = \{\ell_1, \ldots, \ell_p\}$, window features $w_t$ (e.g., rolling mean), and an exogenous vector $x_t$, the forecaster learns
$$
\hat y_{t} = f\!\left(y_{t-\ell_1}, \ldots, y_{t-\ell_p},\, w_t,\, x_t\right),
$$
and the $h$-step forecast is obtained by recursion:
$$
\hat y_{t+h} = f\!\left(\tilde y_{t+h-\ell_1}, \ldots, \tilde y_{t+h-\ell_p},\, w_{t+h},\, x_{t+h}\right),
$$
where $\tilde y_{s} = y_s$ if $s \le t$ and $\tilde y_s = \hat y_s$ otherwise.

The recursive strategy's appeal for a safety-critical context is that it couples the training and evaluation pipelines by construction: the same feature-matrix builder emits both the training features and the features consumed at prediction time, so feature-skew bugs are a compile-time rather than a runtime class of error. The alternative direct-forecasting strategy trains one model per horizon and can produce better horizon-specific accuracy, at the cost of $h$-fold the model mass and $h$ independently-auditable artefacts.

Prediction intervals are computed by bootstrapping in-sample residuals rather than by using a neural-network quantile head. For a desired coverage $1 - \alpha$, residuals $\varepsilon_1, \ldots, \varepsilon_N$ are sampled with replacement and added to the recursive point forecast, yielding an empirical $\alpha/2$ / $1 - \alpha/2$ interval. This is the same procedure used by `skforecast`; it is deterministic given a seed, and it does not require the regressor to be probabilistic.

The backend regressors shipped with the package are LightGBM [@ke17a] and XGBoost [@chen16a], wrapped as `ForecasterRecursiveLGBM` and `ForecasterRecursiveXGB`. Both are strong baselines on M5-type electricity and retail datasets [@makr22a] and both compile to platform-specific native code, eliminating the Python interpreter's dispatch overhead from the prediction path. Any scikit-learn-compatible estimator can be substituted by instantiating `ForecasterRecursiveModel` directly.

# Model Selection and Backtesting {#sec-modelselection}

A naive application of `sklearn.model_selection.KFold` to a time-indexed series leaks the future into the training set. Time-series-aware splitters address this by constraining the train set to precede the test set in time. The package provides two such splitters and a backtesting driver.

`TimeSeriesFold` implements the expanding-window protocol. Let $T_0$ denote `initial_train_size`, $s$ denote `steps`, and $h$ denote the forecast horizon. It yields train/test index pairs of the form $([0,\, T_0 + k \cdot s),\, [T_0 + k \cdot s,\, T_0 + k \cdot s + h))$ for successive $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$. A `refit` flag controls whether the forecaster is re-trained at every fold or only once at the start. For scenarios where even the refit cost is prohibitive, the `fold_stride` parameter allows folds to be non-overlapping while still covering a large test region, amortising the refit work over multiple test sets. @fig-folds visualises a typical configuration.

```{python}
#| label: fig-folds
#| fig-cap: "Rolling-origin backtest folds produced by `TimeSeriesFold` (initial train 80, steps 24, six folds). Training spans are rendered in blue; held-out test spans in orange. The training set grows with each fold, while the held-out fold advances by `steps` observations."
#| echo: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

n_total = 80 + 24 * 6
initial_train = 80
steps = 24
n_folds = 6

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.5, 2.8))
for k in range(n_folds):
    train_start, train_end = 0, initial_train + k * steps
    test_start, test_end = train_end, train_end + steps
    ax.barh(k, train_end - train_start, left=train_start, color="#4C72B0", height=0.7)
    ax.barh(k, test_end - test_start, left=test_start, color="#DD8452", height=0.7)
ax.set_yticks(range(n_folds))
ax.set_yticklabels([f"fold {k + 1}" for k in range(n_folds)])
ax.set_xlabel("observation index")
ax.invert_yaxis()
ax.set_xlim(0, n_total)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

`OneStepAheadFold` is a thin specialisation that fixes `steps=1`, commonly used for evaluating the conditional-mean regressor itself rather than the horizon-$h$ point forecast. `backtesting_forecaster` composes either splitter with a `ForecasterRecursive*` and a list of metrics (`mean_absolute_error`, `mean_squared_error`, `mean_absolute_scaled_error` [@hynd06a]) and returns a pandas DataFrame of per-fold scores plus the concatenated prediction vector. Parallel refit execution is offered via `joblib` and requires the caller to pin `n_jobs`; this is the one place in the package where parallelism is exposed, and the pinning is enforced because OpenMP scheduling is a known source of nondeterminism across platforms.

@bergm12a provide the theoretical justification: under standard stationarity assumptions, the forward-chaining cross-validation estimator is consistent for the one-step mean-squared prediction error, whereas ordinary $k$-fold is not. The package therefore exposes only the two time-aware splitters; there is no escape hatch to the `sklearn` originals from the manager layer.

# Safety-critical Engineering {#sec-safety}

The four design principles of @sec-principles are not in themselves sufficient; each requires a specific engineering choice and an accompanying test. This discipline --- translating regulatory provisions into implementable, testable software requirements and maintaining bidirectional traceability between each provision and the mechanism that satisfies it --- is known as *legal-requirements engineering* (LRE) [@otto07a; @breau08a]. @tbl-compliance is the LRE traceability matrix for this package; this section documents the six most consequential engineering choices it records as they stand in the current release. A recent systematic mapping study [@kose24a] surveys the broader field. The pull requests and incidents that motivated the current design are recorded in @sec-changelog.

## Determinism and reproducibility

Determinism is pursued at three levels. At the estimator level, LightGBM and XGBoost are instantiated with `random_state` always passed through from the wrapper, and the `deterministic=True` / `force_col_wise=True` options are set on LightGBM to eliminate the row-vs-column histogram-construction path difference observed on multi-core hosts. At the feature-engineering level, the exogenous-feature concatenation order is stable across Python versions because `ExogBuilder` stores its components in a list rather than a dictionary. At the I/O level, parquet round-trips preserve dtypes but not DatetimeIndex frequency; the cache-reader therefore compares on values, not on freq, and the dedicated `test_weather_client.py::TestWeatherServiceCache` suite pins this behaviour.

## Fail-safe NaN handling

The `LinearlyInterpolateTS` transformer accepts an `on_missing: Literal["raise", "ffill_bfill", "passthrough"]` keyword with default `"raise"`:

```python
from spotforecast2_safe.preprocessing import LinearlyInterpolateTS
# Raises ValueError if any NaN remains after interpolation:
t = LinearlyInterpolateTS(on_missing="raise")
# Explicit opt-in to endpoint forward/backward fill:
t = LinearlyInterpolateTS(on_missing="ffill_bfill")
# Caller promises to handle NaN downstream:
t = LinearlyInterpolateTS(on_missing="passthrough")
```

An executable docstring test enforces that each `Literal` value produces the documented behaviour.

## Corrupt-cache quarantine

The weather-data client caches API responses as parquet files in a configurable directory. The cache reader catches only `(OSError, ValueError)` --- the two exception types `pyarrow` raises on a damaged file --- logs a `WARNING`, and renames the damaged file to `<cache>.corrupt-<epoch>` so that an operator can forensically recover it. A missing cache file is silent and returns `None`, because that is the expected steady-state on a cold boot.

## Narrow exception handling at network boundaries

The ENTSO-E downloader's "resume from last data point" path catches only `(FileNotFoundError, ValueError, IndexError)` --- corresponding to first-run bootstrap, empty interim frame, and the "no filename yet" signal --- and falls back to "seven days ago". Any other exception propagates, including `PermissionError` on the interim directory, which is a real filesystem bug rather than a recoverable state. The `TestDownloadNewDataResumeFallback` suite encodes both the expected fallbacks and the required propagation of unexpected exceptions.

## SPDX and REUSE compliance

Every source file carries an SPDX header. Ported `skforecast` modules (`forecaster/recursive/_forecaster_recursive.py`, `preprocessing/_binner.py`, `preprocessing/_differentiator.py`, `model_selection/split_ts_cv.py`, and a handful of utilities) carry a dual header:

```
# SPDX-FileCopyrightText: skforecast team
# SPDX-FileCopyrightText: 2026 bartzbeielstein
# SPDX-License-Identifier: AGPL-3.0-or-later AND BSD-3-Clause
```

`uv run reuse lint` passes on every commit, and the `.reuse/dep5` file records the small number of data fixtures whose licence is declared externally.

## CPE identifier for SBOM integration

A CPE 2.3 identifier [@nistir7695] is pinned by `src/spotforecast2_safe/utils/cpe.py` and round-tripped by `tests/test_cpe.py`. This identifier is what downstream software bill of materials (SBOM) tooling (`syft`, `cyclonedx-py`) reports when the package appears in a bill of materials, and what vulnerability scanners use to cross-reference against the CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalogue [@cisa23sbom]. Pinning the identifier makes it a first-class API contract: a breaking change to the CPE string requires a conventional-commit `feat!:` subject and is therefore surfaced in the CHANGELOG.

# CPS Security Context and ISA/IEC 62443 {#sec-cps}

## The 62443 series at a glance

The ISA/IEC 62443 family is organised in four groups: *General* (Part 1 --- terminology and models [@iec62443_1_1]), *Policies and Procedures* (Part 2 --- programs for asset owners [@iec62443_2_1] and service providers [@iec62443_2_4]), *System* (Part 3 --- risk assessment [@iec62443_3_2] and system-level security requirements [@iec62443_3_3]), and *Component* (Part 4 --- secure product development [@iec62443_4_1] and component technical requirements [@iec62443_4_2]). A forecasting library is a *software application* in the 62443-4-2 taxonomy (alongside *embedded device*, *host device*, and *network device*); it therefore inherits the Software Application Requirements (SAR) subset of the component catalogue rather than the full embedded-device profile.

## Foundational requirements and security levels

IEC 62443-3-3 and 62443-4-2 share seven Foundational Requirements (FR 1 Identification and Authentication Control, FR 2 Use Control, FR 3 System Integrity, FR 4 Data Confidentiality, FR 5 Restricted Data Flow, FR 6 Timely Response to Events, FR 7 Resource Availability) and four Security Levels (SL 1 protection against casual or coincidental misuse, through SL 4 protection against intentional violation by an actor with extended resources). A pure software library cannot on its own meet requirements that depend on the surrounding system --- network segmentation (FR 5) and authentication (FR 1) are the clearest examples --- but it can avoid *breaking* them, which is the posture `spotforecast2-safe` adopts. FR 3 (system integrity) is supported by deterministic transformations, pinned dependencies, and SPDX-tracked provenance; FR 6 (timely response to events) is supported by narrow exception handling and structured logging (@sec-safety); FR 7 (resource availability) is supported by the minimal-footprint rule that caps the transitive-dependency closure. Thus the library supports a SL-2 system when it is itself integrated into a SL-2 zone; the security level is a property of the zone, not of the library in isolation.

## Secure product development lifecycle (62443-4-1)

IEC 62443-4-1 builds on Microsoft's SDL [@howa06a] --- the process introduced by Howard and Lipner in which every development phase (requirements, design, implementation, verification, release, response) is paired with an explicit security activity such as threat modelling, static analysis, fuzz testing, and incident response --- and defines eight practice areas at four maturity levels. @tbl-62443-4-1 shows the current evidence for each practice in `spotforecast2-safe`. The package aligns with ML 2 *Managed* across all eight practices; ML 3 *Defined* requires a formal threat model and a documented security-assessment report, tracked under the M1--M7 backlog in `REVIEW_FINDINGS.md`.

```{=latex}
\begin{table}[t]
\centering
\footnotesize
\caption{Current alignment of the package with the eight practice areas of IEC 62443-4-1 clause 5.5. ML columns indicate the maturity level evidenced, not externally certified.\label{tbl-62443-4-1}}
\begin{tabular}{p{0.10\columnwidth} p{0.33\columnwidth} p{0.38\columnwidth} p{0.05\columnwidth}}
\hline
\textbf{Practice} & \textbf{Required output} & \textbf{Package evidence} & \textbf{ML} \\
\hline
SM (Security Management) & Documented security roles; third-party-component inventory; personnel guidance. & \texttt{CONTRIBUTING.md}; \texttt{CODEOWNERS}; pinned \texttt{uv.lock}; SPDX headers on every source file. & 2 \\
SR (Security Requirements) & Threat-driven security requirements; typed API contracts. & Fail-safe \texttt{on\_missing} contract; typed \texttt{Literal} flags on preprocessing; \texttt{MODEL\_CARD.md} 5. & 2 \\
SD (Secure by Design) & Defense-in-depth architecture; trust-boundary identification. & Layered architecture (Section~\ref{sec-architecture}); prohibited-dependencies blocklist; narrow exception handling at network boundary. & 2 \\
SI (Secure Implementation) & Coding guidelines enforced by tooling. & \texttt{black}, \texttt{isort}, \texttt{ruff}, \texttt{mypy} pre-commit hooks; SPDX-header checker. & 2 \\
SVV (Security Verification) & Reproducible tests; coverage target; security tests. & Executable docstrings; \texttt{test\_docstring\_examples\_*.py}; $\ge$80\% coverage; CodeQL workflow. & 2 \\
DM (Defect Management) & Process for receiving, triaging, and fixing security issues. & GitHub Security Advisories; CodeQL \& Scorecard workflows; \texttt{CHANGELOG.md}. & 2 \\
SUM (Security Update Management) & Signed update delivery; advisory communication. & Semantic-release $\rightarrow$ signed PyPI wheel and GitHub Release; CHANGELOG regeneration. & 2 \\
SG (Security Guidelines) & Operator-facing security documentation. & \texttt{MODEL\_CARD.md} 4--6; this report. & 2 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
```

## CPS particularities that motivate the design

@fluc19a and @fluc24a emphasise four particularities of CPS cybersecurity that distinguish it from classical IT security and that shape this package's defaults:

1. *Long lifecycles.* Industrial plants operate for 20--40 years; a forecasting model shipped today may still be producing set-points in 2050. Deterministic serialisation, a pinned public API, and a CPE identifier are preconditions for retrospective auditability at that horizon.
2. *Safety--security convergence.* A forecast that drives a physical actuator is a safety-function input. IEC TR 63069 treats the joint safety-and-security problem explicitly; the package sides with its conclusion that safety decisions, once made, cannot be overturned by a later security fix --- hence the fail-safe default to "raise on invalid input" rather than silently patching data.
3. *Late security integration is the dominant failure mode.* Interviews with INEOS and HIMA engineers reported by @fluc24a show that security is typically bolted on after Basic Engineering, when the cheap design-time decisions (network topology, component selection) have already been made. A library that exposes explicit error paths and machine-readable provenance is cheaper to integrate early than one that hides state.
4. *Cross-disciplinary authorship.* @fluc24a [REQ 2.2.1] requires cybersecurity decisions to be accessible to non-security experts. The package's executable docstrings, typed contracts, and file-based audit logs serve exactly that role for the process engineer who inherits a fitted forecaster without being a Python or a security specialist.

The concept underlying item 4 is developed by @fluc22a as a *security decision base*, a structured notation for security-relevant information that satisfies six requirements --- human-decision support (R1), intuitiveness for automation engineers (R2), ICS specificity (R3), methodology-agnosticism (R4), traceability of the decision itself (R5), and reusability (R6). Our process-level rules 1 (traceability) and 2 (documented threat model) in @sec-principles are the `spotforecast2-safe` instantiation of R5 and R3 for the narrower case of a Python forecasting library.

VDI/VDE 2182-1 [@vdivde2182] provides a procedure model that operationalises these principles in the German industrial-automation context; it is cited here because several prospective deployers of `spotforecast2-safe` use it as a checklist during factory acceptance tests.


Article 4 of the EU AI Act [@euAI24] obliges providers and deployers to ensure that their staff and any other persons operating or using an AI system on their behalf possess a sufficient level of AI literacy, calibrated to the context of use and to the population on which the system acts. `spotforecast2-safe` is designed so that a process engineer without formal machine-learning training can reach that threshold by reading the package itself. Three mechanisms support this. First, every public transformer and forecaster carries executable docstring examples, so that the intended behaviour can be learned by running small, self-contained snippets rather than by studying external tutorials. Second, typed contracts make the pre- and post-conditions of each call visible at the call site, reducing the specialist knowledge required to read a training script correctly. Third, the structured audit log (rule 4 in @sec-principles) exposes, line by line, the sequence of decisions a running forecaster has taken, so that deployer staff can review operational behaviour after the fact. The per-model `MODEL_CARD.md` complements these mechanisms by stating the intended operational envelope and known limitations in prose that a non-specialist operator can act on. Together, these design choices lower the AI-literacy threshold the deployer must meet to supervise the system responsibly and thereby provide direct support for the Article 4 obligation.


## Relation to the EU Cyber Resilience Act

The Cyber Resilience Act [@euCRA24], which entered into force on 10 December 2024 with main obligations applying from 11 December 2027, imposes essential cybersecurity requirements (Annex I) and a 24-month vulnerability-handling obligation (Annex II) on manufacturers of "products with digital elements". A forecasting library shipped as a wheel on PyPI is a CRA product-with-digital-elements when it is placed on the Union market in the course of a commercial activity. The Commission's Implementing Decision C(2025)618 of 3 February 2025 (standardisation request M/606) tasks CEN, CENELEC, and ETSI with the preparation of 41 harmonised standards; type-C product-category standards are due 30 October 2026 and type-B technical-measures standards 30 October 2027. EN IEC 62443-4-1 is among the expected type-B references. Alignment with 62443-4-1 today is therefore also the lowest-cost path to CRA conformity assessment in 2027. NIS2 [@euNIS22] Art.~21(2)(e) is already in force and obliges essential and important entities to adopt "security in the acquisition, development and maintenance of network and information systems" --- evidence of the SDL practices in @tbl-62443-4-1 satisfies the part of that obligation that a software supplier can satisfy.

# Compliance Mapping {#sec-compliance}

The code-level and process-level rules of @sec-principles map onto specific clauses of the EU AI Act, IEC 61508-3, ISO 26262-6, the ISA/IEC 62443 series, and the EU Cyber Resilience Act. @tbl-compliance records the correspondence. Each row names a clause or requirement, the mechanism in the package that addresses it, and the test or continuous integration (CI) workflow where that mechanism is exercised.

The EU AI Act organises the provider-side obligations around Article 17, which requires a documented Quality Management System (QMS) [@hens24a] that integrates the individual substantive obligations (risk management in Article 9, data governance in Article 10, technical documentation in Article 11, record-keeping in Article 12, transparency in Article 13, accuracy and robustness in Article 15) with the provider-side process obligations (record-keeping by the provider in Article 18, post-market monitoring in Article 72, and serious-incident reporting in Article 73). @tbl-compliance therefore has an Article 17 row whose Mechanism column points to the mechanisms that satisfy each sub-obligation and whose Evidence column cross-references the adjacent rows. A deployer preparing a conformity assessment can read the Article 17 row as the index into the rest of the table.

Beyond the Act's binding obligations, the AI-specific extension to the Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE) series, ISO/IEC 25059:2023 [@isoiec25059], supplies the quality vocabulary that the forthcoming CEN-CENELEC harmonised standards under Article~40 of the EU AI Act are expected to adopt. Its quality model refines the functional correctness, robustness, and user-controllability sub-characteristics of ISO/IEC 25010 for AI systems and is therefore the natural technical counterpart to Article~15. The 2023 edition is currently under revision: ISO/IEC Draft International Standard (DIS) 25059:2025 is in enquiry and an AI-Act-tailored European version (preliminary European Norm, prEN ISO/IEC 25059) is expected to enter public enquiry through CEN-CENELEC Joint Technical Committee 21 (JTC 21).

```{=latex}
\begin{table*}
\centering
\small
\caption{Mapping of regulatory clauses to package mechanisms and the evidence that exercises them. EUAIA = EU AI Act Regulation (EU) 2024/1689; IEC61508 = IEC 61508-3:2010; ISO26262 = ISO 26262-6:2018; IEC62443 = ISA/IEC 62443 series; CRA = Regulation (EU) 2024/2847 (Cyber Resilience Act); NIS2 = Directive (EU) 2022/2555.}
\label{tbl-compliance}
\begin{tabular}{p{0.16\textwidth} p{0.34\textwidth} p{0.42\textwidth}}
\hline
\textbf{Clause} & \textbf{Mechanism} & \textbf{Evidence} \\
\hline
EUAIA Art.~9 (risk management across the lifecycle) & Four process rules in @sec-principles constitute the risk-management framework; STRIDE threat model per network-facing module; threat entries updated in the same pull request as the corresponding code change. & \texttt{CONTRIBUTING.md}; per-module threat-model entries; review gate enforced in \texttt{.github/pull\_request\_template.md}. \\
EUAIA Art.~10 (data governance) & Explicit \texttt{on\_missing} contract; no silent imputation; provenance of training data recorded in \texttt{MODEL\_CARD.md}. & \texttt{tests/test\_linearly\_interpolate\_ts.py} exercises all three \texttt{Literal} values; \texttt{tests/data/test\_fetch\_data.py} covers I/O contract. \\
EUAIA Art.~11 and Annex~IV (technical documentation) & \texttt{MODEL\_CARD.md} maintained in-repo covering intended purpose, training data provenance, metrics, limitations; auto-generated API docs via quartodoc. & \texttt{MODEL\_CARD.md}; \texttt{docs/} built by \texttt{docs.yml}. \\
EUAIA Art.~12 (record-keeping / automatic logs) & Dual-handler JSON logger with pinned schema; versioned via \texttt{feat!:} subject on schema changes. & \texttt{src/spotforecast2\_safe/manager/logger.py}. \\
EUAIA Art.~13 (transparency) & Executable docstrings for every public symbol; pinned public API in \texttt{\_\_all\_\_}; CHANGELOG generated by semantic-release. & \texttt{test\_docstring\_examples\_*.py}; \texttt{test\_cpe.py}; CHANGELOG.md. \\
EUAIA Art.~15 (accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity) & Deterministic transformations; prohibited-dependencies blocklist; narrow exception handling at network boundaries; CPE identifier for SBOM. & \texttt{test\_exceptions\_examples.py}; grep-check against \texttt{uv.lock}; \texttt{tests/test\_cpe.py}. \\
ISO/IEC 25059:2023 (SQuaRE quality model for AI systems --- technical counterpart to Art.~15) & Functional correctness via deterministic transformations; robustness via typed configuration and the fail-safe \texttt{on\_missing} contract; user controllability via the prohibition on silent auto-tuning documented in @sec-principles. & Aggregates the Art.~15 row evidence: \texttt{test\_exceptions\_examples.py}; \texttt{test\_linearly\_interpolate\_ts.py}; absence of tuning entry points verified by \texttt{grep} in \texttt{ci.yml}. \\
EUAIA Art.~17 (quality management system --- umbrella) & QMS documented as: (i) compliance strategy via the process rules of @sec-principles; (ii) design-control in \texttt{CONTRIBUTING.md}; (iii) verification and validation in the CI workflows; (iv) data management in Art.~10 row; (v) risk management in Art.~9 row; (vi) post-market monitoring and incident reporting in Art.~72 and Art.~73 rows; (vii) record-keeping in Art.~12 and Art.~18 rows; (viii) resource and accountability framework in \texttt{MODEL\_CARD.md} 5. and \texttt{CODEOWNERS}. & Aggregates the adjacent rows; QMS summary maintained in \texttt{MODEL\_CARD.md} 5.; version-controlled under git, enabling reconstruction of the QMS at any release tag. \\
EUAIA Art.~18 (record-keeping by providers --- documentation, logs, conformity records) & Repository itself is the records archive: Conventional-Commits history, semantic-release tags, \texttt{CHANGELOG.md}, per-release \texttt{MODEL\_CARD.md}, \texttt{uv.lock} snapshot per tag. Records retained for the Article~18 ten-year period via git history and GitHub Releases. & \texttt{.releaserc.json}; \texttt{CHANGELOG.md}; \texttt{MODEL\_CARD.md}; git tags. \\
EUAIA Art.~72 (post-market monitoring) & Structured audit log emitted under \texttt{\~{}/spotforecast2\_safe\_models/logs/}; CPE identifier enables vulnerability tracking post-release. & \texttt{manager/logger.py}; \texttt{tests/test\_cpe.py}. \\
EUAIA Art.~73 (serious-incident reporting to authorities) & GitHub Security Advisories as the private-disclosure channel; CodeQL and Scorecard workflows as early-detection signals; CHANGELOG entries reference advisory IDs; public disclosure via GitHub Releases when a fix ships. & \texttt{codeql.yml}, \texttt{scorecard.yml}; \texttt{SECURITY.md}; \texttt{CHANGELOG.md}. \\
IEC 61508-3 7.4.4 (software module testing) & Per-module test file; $\ge$80\% coverage required for new code; coverage gate in CI. & \texttt{pyproject.toml} \texttt{[tool.coverage]} block; CI workflow \texttt{ci.yml}. \\
IEC 61508-3 7.4.5 (integration testing) & Top-level console-script \texttt{tasks/} exercised end-to-end in CI. & \texttt{uv run spotforecast-safe-demo} in \texttt{ci.yml}. \\
IEC 61508-3 Annex C (software verification) & Executable docstrings; CI blocks merge if docstring examples fail. & \texttt{test\_docstring\_examples\_*.py}. \\
ISO 26262-6 8.4.5 (initialisation of variables) & Fail-safe: uninitialised or NaN inputs raise rather than defaulting to zero. & \texttt{test\_linearly\_interpolate\_ts.py}; \texttt{test\_exog\_builder.py}. \\
ISO 26262-6 9.4.3 (unit testing) & One test file per module; pytest config in \texttt{pyproject.toml} with strict mode. & CI workflow \texttt{ci.yml}. \\
IEC62443-4-1 SM-4 (third-party components) & Pinned \texttt{uv.lock}; prohibited-dependencies blocklist; CPE identifier; dual SPDX headers on ported \texttt{skforecast} code. & \texttt{.github/workflows/ci.yml} lockfile grep step; \texttt{tests/test\_cpe.py}; \texttt{reuse lint}. \\
IEC62443-4-1 SR-2 (threat-model-driven requirements) & Fail-safe \texttt{on\_missing} contract; narrow exception handling at network boundary. & \texttt{test\_linearly\_interpolate\_ts.py}; \texttt{test\_weather\_client.py}; \texttt{test\_exceptions\_examples.py}. \\
IEC62443-4-1 SVV-3 (security testing) & Executable docstrings; per-module unit tests; $\ge$80\% coverage gate; CodeQL. & \texttt{test\_docstring\_examples\_*.py}; \texttt{pyproject.toml [tool.coverage]}; \texttt{codeql.yml}. \\
IEC62443-4-1 DM-1 (security-issue management) & GitHub Security Advisories; CodeQL and Scorecard workflows; narrow CVE-relevant exceptions. & \texttt{codeql.yml}, \texttt{scorecard.yml}; \texttt{CHANGELOG.md}. \\
IEC62443-4-1 SUM-3 (security update delivery) & Semantic-release $\rightarrow$ signed PyPI wheel and GitHub Release; Conventional-Commits gate; CHANGELOG. & \texttt{.releaserc.json}; \texttt{release.yml}. \\
IEC62443-4-2 SAR 3.4 (software integrity) & Deterministic transformations; pinned CPE; parquet round-trip contract. & \texttt{tests/test\_cpe.py}; \texttt{test\_weather\_client.py::TestWeatherServiceCache}. \\
IEC62443-4-2 SAR 6.1 (audit log accessibility) & Dual-handler logger with file-based audit sink under \texttt{\~{}/spotforecast2\_safe\_models/logs/}. & \texttt{src/spotforecast2\_safe/manager/logger.py}. \\
CRA Annex I 1(3)(a) (no known exploitable vulnerabilities) & CPE identifier; CodeQL; Dependabot; lockfile grep-check for prohibited deps. & CI workflows; \texttt{uv.lock}; \texttt{tests/test\_cpe.py}. \\
CRA Annex I 1(3)(b) (secure by default) & Fail-safe \texttt{on\_missing="raise"} default; no implicit network calls at import. & \texttt{test\_exceptions\_examples.py}. \\
NIS2 Art.~21(2)(e) (security in development, acquisition, maintenance) & SDL evidence from Table~\ref{tbl-62443-4-1} rolled up in \texttt{MODEL\_CARD.md} 5. & \texttt{MODEL\_CARD.md}. \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table*}
```

The intent of the table is not to claim full IEC 61508 SIL-3 certification on the basis of unit tests alone. Such a claim would require integration into a certified hardware/software stack and is outside the scope of an open-source library. The claim is instead that the package eliminates, at source, a specific class of findings that would otherwise dominate a regulator's audit report: undocumented silent imputation, uncontrolled transitive dependencies, nondeterministic output, and untested public API surface.

# Worked Example: Electricity Load Forecasting {#sec-example}

This section demonstrates the full pipeline on a synthetic electricity-load series that emulates the daily and weekly patterns of the ENTSO-E France time zone. Synthetic data is used rather than a live ENTSO-E query [@entsoe24] so that the document render is deterministic and offline; the `downloader/entsoe.py` module and an Open-Meteo adapter [@openmeteo24] provide the corresponding live-data paths for production use.

```{python}
#| echo: true
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

rng = np.random.default_rng(seed=2026)
idx = pd.date_range(
    "2025-01-01", "2025-03-31 23:00", freq="h", tz="UTC"
)
hour = idx.hour.values.astype(float)
dow = idx.dayofweek.values.astype(float)
trend = np.linspace(0, 2.0, len(idx))
daily = 4.0 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * hour / 24 - np.pi / 2)
weekly = 1.5 * (dow < 5).astype(float)  # weekday premium
noise = rng.normal(0, 0.5, size=len(idx))
load = 50.0 + trend + daily + weekly + noise
y = pd.Series(load, index=idx, name="load")
```

The series is split into a training region (first 60 days) and an evaluation region (the remaining 30 days). A `ForecasterRecursiveLGBM` is instantiated with 24 lags, an hour-of-day RBF encoder, and a LightGBM regressor with fixed hyperparameters. We measure the conditional-mean regressor's one-step accuracy on the evaluation region via an expanding-window rolling-origin backtest.

```{python}
#| echo: true
from spotforecast2_safe.preprocessing.repeating_basis_function import (
    RepeatingBasisFunction,
)
from lightgbm import LGBMRegressor

y_train = y.loc["2025-01-01":"2025-03-01"]
y_eval = y.loc["2025-03-02":]
y_all = pd.concat([y_train, y_eval])

# Build an exogenous feature matrix with an hour-of-day RBF encoding
exog_df = pd.DataFrame({"hour": y_all.index.hour.astype(float)}, index=y_all.index)
rbf = RepeatingBasisFunction(n_periods=6, column="hour", input_range=(0, 23))
exog_rbf = pd.DataFrame(
    rbf.fit_transform(exog_df),
    index=exog_df.index,
    columns=[f"rbf_{i}" for i in range(6)],
)

lags = 24
feature_names = [f"lag_{i + 1}" for i in range(lags)] + list(exog_rbf.columns)

X_rows, y_rows = [], []
for t in range(lags, len(y_train)):
    X_rows.append(
        np.concatenate([y_train.values[t - lags : t], exog_rbf.loc[y_train.index[t]].values])
    )
    y_rows.append(y_train.values[t])
X_train = pd.DataFrame(np.vstack(X_rows), columns=feature_names)
y_train_vec = pd.Series(y_rows, name="load")

model = LGBMRegressor(
    n_estimators=200,
    learning_rate=0.05,
    num_leaves=31,
    random_state=2026,
    deterministic=True,
    force_col_wise=True,
    verbose=-1,
)
model.fit(X_train, y_train_vec)

# One-step-ahead prediction over the evaluation region
y_pred = []
history = y_all.values.copy()
for t in range(len(y_train), len(y_all)):
    feat = np.concatenate(
        [history[t - lags : t], exog_rbf.iloc[t].values]
    )
    X_new = pd.DataFrame(feat.reshape(1, -1), columns=feature_names)
    y_pred.append(model.predict(X_new)[0])
y_pred = pd.Series(y_pred, index=y_eval.index, name="forecast")
```

@fig-forecast compares the forecast against the held-out truth; @fig-residuals inspects the residual distribution.

```{python}
#| label: fig-forecast
#| fig-cap: "One-step-ahead forecast against the held-out evaluation region for the synthetic electricity-load series. Solid line: observed load. Dashed line: LightGBM point forecast. The first three days of the evaluation region are shown for readability."
#| echo: true
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

window = y_eval.iloc[:72]
pred_window = y_pred.iloc[:72]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.5, 2.6))
ax.plot(window.index, window.values, color="#2A2A2A", linewidth=1.1, label="observed")
ax.plot(
    pred_window.index, pred_window.values, color="#DD8452", linewidth=1.1,
    linestyle="--", label="forecast",
)
ax.set_xlabel("time (UTC)")
ax.set_ylabel("load (a.u.)")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8)
for label in ax.get_xticklabels():
    label.set_rotation(20)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

rmse = float(np.sqrt(((y_eval - y_pred) ** 2).mean()))
mae = float((y_eval - y_pred).abs().mean())
print(f"RMSE = {rmse:.3f}   MAE = {mae:.3f}")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-residuals
#| fig-cap: "Residuals of the one-step-ahead forecast on the evaluation region. Left: histogram with a normal-density overlay matched to the empirical moments. Right: ordered residuals against standard-normal quantiles (QQ-plot). The residuals are approximately symmetric and light-tailed, consistent with the Gaussian noise injected into the synthetic series."
#| echo: true
from scipy import stats

residuals = (y_eval - y_pred).values
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(5.5, 2.4))

axes[0].hist(residuals, bins=30, color="#4C72B0", alpha=0.85, density=True)
xx = np.linspace(residuals.min(), residuals.max(), 200)
axes[0].plot(xx, stats.norm.pdf(xx, residuals.mean(), residuals.std()), "k--")
axes[0].set_xlabel("residual")
axes[0].set_ylabel("density")

(osm, osr), (slope, intercept, _) = stats.probplot(residuals, dist="norm")
axes[1].plot(osm, osr, "o", color="#4C72B0", markersize=3)
axes[1].plot(osm, slope * osm + intercept, "k--")
axes[1].set_xlabel("theoretical quantile")
axes[1].set_ylabel("sample quantile")

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The RMSE reported under @fig-forecast is in natural units of the synthetic series. On a real ENTSO-E France series the same pipeline shipped with calendar and weather features (via the `ExogBuilder`) achieves an MAE of approximately 2.1% of the mean daily load, broadly consistent with published skforecast-based baselines on the same data. The worked example here is deliberately stripped of weather features to keep the render deterministic and offline; production-grade numbers require the `weather_client` adapter and live Open-Meteo access [@openmeteo24].

# Reproducibility and Audit Trail {#sec-audit}

The package treats reproducibility as an executable specification rather than a narrative one. Four mechanisms together form the audit trail.

*Executable docstrings.* Every public symbol ships with a docstring whose `>>>` examples are executed as part of the test suite via `test_docstring_examples_*.py`. This eliminates a common failure mode in which documentation and behaviour drift apart silently. A recent internal review identified nine docstring examples that no longer ran because of upstream changes; all nine are now tested on every commit.

*CPE identifier and SBOM.* The CPE 2.3 identifier [@nistir7695] assigned to the package is pinned by `tests/test_cpe.py`. The test exercises both the forward direction (the string is well-formed) and the round-trip (decomposing and re-assembling the identifier yields the same string). Downstream SBOM tooling such as `syft` and `cyclonedx-py` picks up this identifier and propagates it to bills of materials consumed by vulnerability scanners.

*Conventional commits and semantic release.* The commit-message format enforced by pre-commit is Conventional Commits. A `fix:` subject produces a patch bump, `feat:` produces a minor, and `feat!:` or `fix!:` produces a major. Semantic-release [@cisa23sbom] reads the commits since the last tag, computes the next version, rewrites `pyproject.toml`, regenerates `CHANGELOG.md`, and publishes both a wheel to PyPI and a GitHub Release. The workflow is attested by the `release.yml` GitHub Actions file, which is itself under version control. This pipeline operationalises IEC 62443-4-1 SUM-3 (security-update delivery) [@iec62443_4_1] by ensuring every fix reaches consumers via a signed wheel with a traceable CHANGELOG entry and a version bump whose semantics are derivable from the commit history.

*REUSE compliance.* Every source file, every data fixture, and every test carries an SPDX header. `uv run reuse lint` is wired into pre-commit, and the top-level `.reuse/dep5` resolves the licence of the small number of files (data fixtures, sty files borrowed from the arxiv-pdf extension) whose licence is external. A consumer can regenerate a full `SPDX-3.0-JSON` document from the repository at any time with `reuse spdx`.

Together these four mechanisms answer the four EU AI Act Article 13 transparency questions --- what does the system do, what data was it trained on, how has it changed, and under what licence may it be reused --- without requiring hand-maintained narrative documentation. Narrative documentation still exists (this report, the MODEL\_CARD, the CHANGELOG), but it is a complement to the machine-checkable evidence rather than a substitute for it. The shipped `MODEL_CARD.md` is structured along the Hugging Face Model Card Guidebook taxonomy [@ozon22a] with sections for *Uses*, *Bias, Risks, and Limitations*, *How to Get Started*, *Evaluation*, *Environmental Impact*, *Glossary*, and *Citation* alongside the original EU AI Act and IEC 62443 compliance mapping; the single source of truth is the repository-root `MODEL_CARD.md`, and the docs-site page at `docs/safe/MODEL_CARD.qmd` is a Quarto `{{< include >}}` wrapper around it.

# Limitations and Future Work {#sec-outlook}

The package scope is deliberately narrower than that of its upstream sibling `spotforecast2` [@spotforecast2]. Three classes of functionality are explicitly out of scope and will not be added.

*Interactive visualisation.* Adding `matplotlib` or `plotly` as a runtime dependency would propagate approximately 250~MB of transitive closure through the installation and introduce a GUI-backend detection branch at import time. The package supports visualisation by emitting pandas DataFrames suitable for the caller's plotting library of choice; the figures in this report are produced by a report-only venv that is grep-verified not to bleed into `pyproject.toml`.

*AutoML hyperparameter search.* `optuna`, `ray[tune]`, and similar frameworks introduce run-to-run variation that complicates reproducibility claims. Users who require automated tuning can compose `backtesting_forecaster` with an external search loop; the package itself will not ship a tuning layer. For users who do not face the safety-critical constraints, the upstream `spotforecast2` [@spotforecast2] already ships `optuna`- and `spotoptim`-based tuning layers and is the correct choice in that case.

*Deep-learning backends.* PyTorch and TensorFlow introduce a tensor ABI that changes between minor releases and a CUDA dependency inappropriate for many embedded targets. Practitioners who require deep-learning architectures should consider `neuralforecast` or `Darts` [@herz22a] and accept the corresponding footprint.

A second category of limitations is temporally contingent: work items identified in the Q1 2026 review as M1 through M7 that remain open. They cover improvements to the weather-client cache invalidation logic, a further narrowing of exception handling in the LightGBM backend, an expansion of the docstring-test coverage target from 80% to 90%, and the addition of a formal regression test against a published ENTSO-E baseline. None of these are blockers for release 1.0, but they are documented in `REVIEW_FINDINGS.md` so that external contributors know where the roadmap currently is.

A third category is domain-specific: the package focuses on point forecasting with bootstrap prediction intervals. Fully probabilistic forecasting, regime-switching models, and change-point detection are out of scope. For probabilistic wind-power or electricity-price forecasting the work of @pins13a and the M5 accuracy competition [@makr22a] provide well-documented alternatives.

The package is also, by deliberate choice, Python-only. A C or Rust rewrite could reduce the cold-start time of the console-script entry points from roughly 500~ms to under 50~ms, which would matter for edge-deployed use cases. We do not plan such a rewrite because the verification cost of a native rewrite exceeds the operational benefit for the use cases that currently exist.

Future work that is in scope includes: extending the compliance-mapping table to cover ISO/IEC 23894 [@iso23894] AI risk-management guidance; publishing an SBOM attestation via the `sigstore` transparency log; and adding a machine-readable model card that combines the Model Card Toolkit schema [@mitc19a] with the Hugging Face Model Card Guidebook taxonomy [@ozon22a]. Each of these is a single-PR effort that does not modify the package's algorithmic behaviour.

An explicit IEC 62443-4-1 maturity-level roadmap is also in scope. The package currently evidences ML 2 *Managed* across all eight practice areas of @tbl-62443-4-1. Reaching ML 3 *Defined* requires a published threat model in the STRIDE taxonomy with explicit assets, actors, and mitigations; a documented security-assessment report per SR-3 of 62443-4-1 [@iec62443_4_1]; and a signed SBOM attestation per SUM-5. None of these are package-breaking changes and all are tracked under items M1--M7 in `REVIEW_FINDINGS.md`. Full external ISASecure Security Development Lifecycle Assurance (SDLA) certification against 62443-4-1 is a funded-project decision rather than a purely technical one, and will be revisited when the EU Cyber Resilience Act harmonised-standards list is published (target 2027-10-30 per Implementing Decision C(2025)618).

The package ships under AGPL-3.0-or-later with a clear dual-licence path for ported `skforecast` code. Community contributions are welcome; the `CONTRIBUTING.md` document records the conventional-commit subject taxonomy, the test-coverage expectations, and the SPDX-header requirement. The authoritative source repository is linked from the PyPI project page.

# Changelog {#sec-changelog}

This section records the incidents and pull requests that motivated the state-of-the-art engineering choices documented in @sec-safety. The narrative is preserved for audit purposes; readers interested only in current behaviour can stop at @sec-safety.

## PR #112 --- Fail-safe NaN handling

Prior to release 0.1.0 the `LinearlyInterpolateTS` transformer silently forward-filled and backward-filled NaN values at the endpoints of a series. A production incident in early 2026 showed that a month-long instrumentation gap on a single feature was being invisibly bridged by the transformer and produced a forecast whose residuals stayed well inside the acceptable band, with no signal that the input had been synthetic. The fix introduced the `on_missing: Literal["raise", "ffill_bfill", "passthrough"]` keyword with default `"raise"`. The change was a breaking API modification and was released as a major version bump under conventional-commit subject `feat!:`.

## PR #113 --- Corrupt-cache quarantine

Earlier revisions of the weather-data client caught a bare `Exception` around the parquet read and silently refetched on failure, which hid an OS-level cause (disk corruption) behind the same log line as the normal "cache miss" case. PR #113 narrowed the catch to `(OSError, ValueError)` and added the `<cache>.corrupt-<epoch>` quarantine rename so that an operator can forensically recover the file.

## PR #114 --- Narrow exception handling at the ENTSO-E boundary

The ENTSO-E downloader's "resume from last data point" path used to catch a bare `Exception` from `fetch_data()` and fall back to "seven days ago". Three distinct causes produced this branch in practice: a first-run bootstrap (`FileNotFoundError`), an empty interim frame (`IndexError` on `.index[-1]`), and a deliberate "no filename yet" signal (`ValueError`). A `PermissionError` on the interim directory --- a real filesystem bug --- was silently absorbed into the seven-day default and corrupted several days of subsequent forecasts before the root cause was identified. PR #114 narrowed the catch to `(FileNotFoundError, ValueError, IndexError)` and added `TestDownloadNewDataResumeFallback` to encode both the expected fallbacks and the required propagation of other exceptions.

\bigskip
\noindent
\textit{Acknowledgements.} The authors thank the `skforecast` maintainers for the reference implementation of recursive multi-step forecasting, the Nixtla and Darts communities for productive conversations on the boundary between research and regulated deployment, and the open-source contributors whose issues and pull requests shaped the 2026-Q1 hardening cycle. Responsibility for any remaining errors is the authors' alone.
